title: Min stack (no extra space)
description: Stack with getMin in O(1) without extra space

examples.input: push(5), push(3), push(7), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize stack: []. Step 2: push(5): stack becomes [5]. Step 3: push(3): stack becomes [5, 3]. Step 4: push(7): stack becomes [5, 3, 7]. Step 5: getMin(): iterate through [5, 3, 7], min is 3, return 3. Step 6: pop(): remove 7, stack becomes [5, 3], return 7. Step 7: getMin(): iterate through [5, 3], min is 3, return 3.

examples.input: push(10), push(20), getMin(), push(5), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize stack: []. Step 2: push(10): stack becomes [10]. Step 3: push(20): stack becomes [10, 20]. Step 4: getMin(): iterate through [10, 20], min is 10, return 10. Step 5: push(5): stack becomes [10, 20, 5]. Step 6: getMin(): iterate through [10, 20, 5], min is 5, return 5. Step 7: pop(): remove 5, stack becomes [10, 20], return 5. Step 8: getMin(): iterate through [10, 20], min is 10, return 10.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n) per getMin
approaches.summary: Implement a standard stack, and for getMin, scan all elements to find the minimum each time. This is straightforward but inefficient for frequent getMin calls.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n) for getMin, O(1) for push/pop

ladders.title: Full Problem — Min Stack with O(n) getMin
ladders.desc: Implement a stack that supports push, pop, top, and getMin operations, where getMin scans all elements to find minimum.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires implementing a stack data structure with an additional getMin operation that returns the minimum element. In this brute force approach, getMin runs in linear time by scanning the entire stack each time it is called. The intuition is straightforward: maintain a standard stack using an array or linked list, and when getMin is requested, iterate through all elements to find the smallest value. This approach is inefficient for frequent getMin calls but requires no extra space beyond the stack itself. The algorithm involves: 1) Initialize an empty list for the stack. 2) For push(val), append val to the list. 3) For pop(), remove and return the last element if stack is not empty. 4) For top(), return the last element without removal. 5) For getMin(), iterate through all elements, track the minimum, and return it. Time complexity: O(1) for push, pop, top; O(n) for getMin where n is stack size. Space complexity: O(n) for storing elements. Connection to higher levels: This is the complete implementation of the brute force approach. Common mistakes include not handling empty stack cases, incorrect iteration bounds, or modifying the stack during getMin. Real code pattern: class MinStack: def __init__(self): self.stack = []; def push(self, val): self.stack.append(val); def pop(self): if self.stack: return self.stack.pop(); def top(self): if self.stack: return self.stack[-1]; def getMin(self): if not self.stack: return None; min_val = self.stack[0]; for val in self.stack: if val < min_val: min_val = val; return min_val.

examples.input: push(5), push(3), push(7), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize stack: []. Step 2: push(5): stack becomes [5]. Step 3: push(3): stack becomes [5, 3]. Step 4: push(7): stack becomes [5, 3, 7]. Step 5: getMin(): iterate through [5, 3, 7], min is 3, return 3. Step 6: pop(): remove 7, stack becomes [5, 3], return 7. Step 7: getMin(): iterate through [5, 3], min is 3, return 3.

examples.input: push(10), push(20), getMin(), push(5), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize stack: []. Step 2: push(10): stack becomes [10]. Step 3: push(20): stack becomes [10, 20]. Step 4: getMin(): iterate through [10, 20], min is 10, return 10. Step 5: push(5): stack becomes [10, 20, 5]. Step 6: getMin(): iterate through [10, 20, 5], min is 5, return 5. Step 7: pop(): remove 5, stack becomes [10, 20], return 5. Step 8: getMin(): iterate through [10, 20], min is 10, return 10.

testCases.input: push(5), push(1), push(3), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(7), push(7), push(7), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(-1), push(0), push(-2), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(100), push(50), push(75), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(3), push(2), push(1), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(0), push(-1), push(1), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(5), push(3), push(4), push(2), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin()

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Linear Scan for Minimum in Array
ladders.desc: Implement a function that scans an array to find the minimum element.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the getMin operation in the brute force approach. The intuition is simple: to find the smallest value in a collection, we need to examine each element once and keep track of the minimum seen so far. This is a fundamental search operation. The algorithm: 1) If the array is empty, return None or handle appropriately. 2) Initialize min_val to the first element. 3) Iterate through each element in the array. 4) For each element, if it is less than min_val, update min_val. 5) After iteration, return min_val. Time complexity: O(n) where n is array length, as we visit each element once. Space complexity: O(1) extra space, only using a variable for min_val. Connection to level above: This function is called within getMin() to scan the stack array. Common mistakes include starting min_val with 0 or a large number instead of the first element, which fails for negative values or empty arrays. Also, off-by-one errors in iteration bounds. Real code pattern: def find_min(arr): if not arr: return None; min_val = arr[0]; for val in arr: if val < min_val: min_val = val; return min_val.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array is [5, 3, 7], not empty. Step 2: Initialize min_val = first element = 5. Step 3: Iterate: val=5, 5 < 5? No, min_val stays 5. Step 4: val=3, 3 < 5? Yes, update min_val = 3. Step 5: val=7, 7 < 3? No, min_val stays 3. Step 6: Return min_val = 3.

Step 1: Array is [10, 20, 5, 15], not empty. Step 2: Initialize min_val = 10. Step 3: Iterate: val=10, 10 < 10? No, min_val=10. Step 4: val=20, 20 < 10? No, min_val=10. Step 5: val=5, 5 < 10? Yes, update min_val = 5. Step 6: val=15, 15 < 5? No, min_val=5. Step 7: Return min_val = 5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Comparison for Minimum Tracking
ladders.desc: Understand how to track the minimum value while iterating through a sequence.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the technique of maintaining a running minimum during iteration. The intuition is that we can find the minimum in a list by comparing each element with the current minimum and updating it if a smaller element is found. This is efficient because it requires only one pass through the data. The algorithm: Start with an initial minimum (e.g., the first element or a sentinel value). For each subsequent element, compare it to the current minimum. If the element is smaller, it becomes the new minimum. This process ensures that after checking all elements, the minimum is correctly identified. Time complexity: O(n) for the iteration. Space complexity: O(1) for storing the current minimum. Connection to level above: This logic is implemented in the linear scan function. Common mistakes include not initializing the minimum properly (e.g., using 0 for all-positive lists, which fails for negatives), or skipping the first element in comparison. Real code pattern: min_val = arr[0]; for i in range(1, len(arr)): if arr[i] < min_val: min_val = arr[i]; return min_val.

examples.input: Sequence: 5, 3, 7
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with min_val = 5 (first element). Step 2: Compare with next element 3: 3 < 5? Yes, update min_val = 3. Step 3: Compare with next element 7: 7 < 3? No, min_val stays 3. Step 4: After iteration, min_val = 3.

examples.input: Sequence: 10, 20, 5, 15
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with min_val = 10. Step 2: Compare with 20: 20 < 10? No, min_val=10. Step 3: Compare with 5: 5 < 10? Yes, update min_val = 5. Step 4: Compare with 15: 15 < 5? No, min_val=5. Step 5: After iteration, min_val = 5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn how to iterate through an array using indices or iterators.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the fundamental skill of traversing an array to access each element. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we can use indices to sequentially visit each position. This is essential for operations like searching or processing all elements. The algorithm: For an array of length n, use a loop (e.g., for or while) to iterate from index 0 to n-1. At each index, access the element using array[index]. This allows performing operations on each element, such as comparison or summation. Time complexity: O(n) for traversing all n elements. Space complexity: O(1) if no extra data structures are used. Connection to level above: Traversal is used in the iterative comparison logic to check each element. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., going beyond n-1), or modifying the array during traversal which can cause unexpected behavior. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): current_element = arr[i]; # process current_element.

examples.input: Array: [5, 3, 7], traverse and print each element
examples.trace: Step 1: Array length = 3. Step 2: i=0, access arr[0]=5, print 5. Step 3: i=1, access arr[1]=3, print 3. Step 4: i=2, access arr[2]=7, print 7. Step 5: Loop ends as i reaches 3.

examples.input: Array: [10, 20, 5, 15], traverse and sum elements
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize sum=0. Step 2: i=0, arr[0]=10, sum=0+10=10. Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=20, sum=10+20=30. Step 4: i=2, arr[2]=5, sum=30+5=35. Step 5: i=3, arr[3]=15, sum=35+15=50. Step 6: Loop ends, sum=50.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Learn how to retrieve an element from an array using its position (index).
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing a specific element in an array given its index. The intuition is that arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. To access an element, use the syntax array[index], which returns the value stored at that position. This is fundamental for reading or modifying data in arrays. The algorithm: 1) Ensure the index is within bounds (0 to length-1). 2) Use the index to retrieve the element. Time complexity: O(1) for direct access. Space complexity: O(1) extra space. Connection to level above: Indexing is used during array traversal to get each element. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (common in some languages) or accessing out-of-bounds indices, which causes errors. Real code pattern: element = arr[index]; # where index is an integer.

examples.input: Array: [5, 3, 7], index: 1
examples.trace: Step 1: Array is [5, 3, 7]. Step 2: Index 1 is within bounds (0 to 2). Step 3: Access arr[1] = 3. Step 4: Return 3.
Step 1: Array is [10, 20, 5, 15]. Step 2: Index 2 is within bounds (0 to 3). Step 3: Access arr[2] = 5. Step 4: Return 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays as Lists
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how to create and use a simple list of elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces arrays, which are ordered collections of elements stored in contiguous memory locations. In Python, arrays are often implemented as lists. The intuition is that an array allows you to group multiple values under a single variable name, making it easy to store and manage data. To create an array, you can initialize it with elements, e.g., arr = [1, 2, 3]. Basic operations include: adding elements (append), removing elements (pop), and accessing by position. Arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at position 0. This is crucial for all higher-level operations. Time complexity for basic operations: O(1) for access, O(1) amortized for append/pop at end. Space complexity: O(n) for storing n elements. Connection to level above: Arrays are the data structure used to implement the stack in the min stack problem. Common mistakes include confusing arrays with other data structures or misunderstanding indexing. Real code pattern: arr = []; arr.append(5); # arr becomes [5]; first_element = arr[0]; # returns 5.

examples.input: Create an array with elements 5, 3, 7
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize an empty array: arr = []. Step 2: Add 5: arr.append(5) -> arr = [5]. Step 3: Add 3: arr.append(3) -> arr = [5, 3]. Step 4: Add 7: arr.append(7) -> arr = [5, 3, 7]. Step 5: The array is now [5, 3, 7].

examples.input: Create an array with 10, 20, 5, 15 and access the third element
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize array: arr = [10, 20, 5, 15]. Step 2: Arrays are zero-indexed: indices are 0:10, 1:20, 2:5, 3:15. Step 3: The third element corresponds to index 2 (since first is index 0). Step 4: Access arr[2] = 5. Step 5: Return 5.

testCases.input: Create array [5, 1, 3] and get second element
Create array [-1, 0, -2] and remove last element
Create array [100, 50, 75] and access first element
Create empty array and check if empty
Create array [5, 3, 4, 2] and get last element

approaches.name: Store Min as Difference O(1) extra space
approaches.summary: Store the difference between each element and the current minimum in the stack, updating the minimum on push and pop. This allows O(1) getMin without extra data structures.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space beyond stack storage

ladders.title: Full Problem — Min Stack with O(1) Extra Space Using Difference Storage
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a stack supporting push, pop, top, and getMin operations with O(1) time and O(1) extra space using difference encoding.
ladders.explanation:
This solution implements a stack that can retrieve the minimum element in O(1) time while using only O(1) extra space beyond the stack storage. The core insight is to store the difference between each element and the current minimum rather than the actual element values. This allows us to reconstruct both the original value and track the minimum efficiently.

INTUITION: Instead of maintaining a separate stack for minimum values (which would require O(n) extra space), we encode information about the minimum within the stored values themselves. When we push a new element, we calculate its difference from the current minimum. If this difference is negative (meaning the new element is smaller than current min), we update the minimum. The actual stored value becomes this difference. When popping, if the popped difference is negative, it indicates we're removing the current minimum, so we need to restore the previous minimum by reversing the calculation.

ALGORITHM:
1. Initialize: stack = empty list, min_val = None
2. Push(val):
   - If stack is empty: push 0, min_val = val
   - Else: diff = val - min_val
     - If diff < 0: push diff, update min_val = val
     - Else: push diff
3. Pop():
   - diff = stack.pop()
   - If diff < 0: min_val = min_val - diff  # Restore previous min
4. Top():
   - diff = stack[-1]
   - If diff < 0: return min_val  # Current min is at top
   - Else: return min_val + diff  # Reconstruct original value
5. getMin(): return min_val

TIME/SPACE: All operations O(1) time. O(1) extra space beyond the stack storage.

CONNECTION TO L1: This full solution integrates the key encoding/decoding subroutine from L1 with stack operations.

COMMON MISTAKES:
1. Forgetting to handle empty stack cases
2. Incorrectly reconstructing top value when diff is negative
3. Not updating min_val correctly during pop when diff is negative

CODE PATTERN:
class MinStack:
    def __init__(self):
        self.stack = []
        self.min_val = None
    
    def push(self, val):
        if not self.stack:
            self.stack.append(0)
            self.min_val = val
        else:
            diff = val - self.min_val
            self.stack.append(diff)
            if diff < 0:
                self.min_val = val
    
    def pop(self):
        diff = self.stack.pop()
        if diff < 0:
            self.min_val = self.min_val - diff
        if not self.stack:
            self.min_val = None
    
    def top(self):
        diff = self.stack[-1]
        if diff < 0:
            return self.min_val
        return self.min_val + diff
    
    def getMin(self):
        return self.min_val

examples.input: Operations: push(5), push(3), push(7), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), top()
examples.output: getMin() after pushes: 3, getMin() after pop: 3, top() after pop: 3
examples.trace:
Initialize: stack=[], min_val=None
push(5): stack empty, push 0, min_val=5 → stack=[0], min_val=5
push(3): diff=3-5=-2 (<0), push -2, update min_val=3 → stack=[0,-2], min_val=3
push(7): diff=7-3=4 (≥0), push 4 → stack=[0,-2,4], min_val=3
getMin(): return min_val=3
pop(): diff=4 (≥0), pop 4 → stack=[0,-2], min_val=3
getMin(): return min_val=3
top(): diff=-2 (<0), return min_val=3

examples.input: Operations: push(10), push(20), push(5), push(15), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), top()
examples.output: getMin() after pushes: 5, getMin() after first pop: 5, getMin() after second pop: 10, top() after pops: 20
examples.trace:
Initialize: stack=[], min_val=None
push(10): stack empty, push 0, min_val=10 → stack=[0], min_val=10
push(20): diff=20-10=10, push 10 → stack=[0,10], min_val=10
push(5): diff=5-10=-5, push -5, update min_val=5 → stack=[0,10,-5], min_val=5
push(15): diff=15-5=10, push 10 → stack=[0,10,-5,10], min_val=5
pop(): diff=10, pop 10 → stack=[0,10,-5], min_val=5
getMin(): return 5
pop(): diff=-5 (<0), pop -5, restore min_val=5-(-5)=10 → stack=[0,10], min_val=10
getMin(): return 10
top(): diff=10 (≥0), return min_val+diff=10+10=20

testCases.input: push(5), push(3), push(7), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(10), push(20), push(5), push(15), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(2), push(0), push(3), push(0), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(-2), push(0), push(-3), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(1), push(2), push(3), push(4), push(5), getMin(), pop(), pop(), getMin()
push(5), pop(), push(3), push(1), push(4), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(100), push(50), push(75), push(25), push(80), getMin(), pop(), pop(), getMin()
push(0), push(-1), push(-2), push(-1), push(0), getMin(), pop(), pop(), getMin()
push(7), push(7), push(7), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin()

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Encoding and Decoding Values Using Difference from Minimum
ladders.desc: Implement the core encoding/decoding logic that stores differences and reconstructs original values.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine forms the heart of the min stack solution. It handles how we encode values when pushing (storing difference from current minimum) and decode values when accessing (reconstructing original value from stored difference).

INTUITION: The key insight is that we don't need to store actual values - we can store information about their relationship to the current minimum. When pushing a value smaller than current minimum, we store a negative difference (indicating a new minimum) and update the minimum. When pushing a larger value, we store a positive difference. This encoding lets us reconstruct the original value when needed by adding the difference back to the current minimum.

ALGORITHM:
Encoding (during push):
1. If stack empty: store 0, set min = val
2. Else: diff = val - current_min
   - Store diff
   - If diff < 0: update current_min = val

Decoding (for top operation):
1. Retrieve stored diff
2. If diff < 0: original value = current_min (this was the minimum when pushed)
3. Else: original value = current_min + diff

TIME/SPACE: O(1) time for both encode and decode. O(1) space for the difference storage.

CONNECTION TO L0: This encoding/decoding logic is exactly what enables the full min stack implementation with O(1) extra space.

COMMON MISTAKES:
1. Confusing when to use current_min vs original value in decoding
2. Forgetting that negative diff means the stored value WAS the minimum at push time
3. Incorrectly calculating the difference (val - min, not min - val)

CODE PATTERN:
def encode_push(val, stack, min_val):
    if not stack:
        stack.append(0)
        min_val = val
    else:
        diff = val - min_val
        stack.append(diff)
        if diff < 0:
            min_val = val
    return min_val

def decode_top(stack, min_val):
    diff = stack[-1]
    if diff < 0:
        return min_val
    return min_val + diff

examples.input: Current min=5, encode value=3, then decode
examples.trace:
Encoding: diff = 3-5 = -2 (negative, indicates new min)
Store: -2
Update min: min becomes 3
Decoding: Retrieve diff=-2
Since diff<0: decoded value = current min = 3

examples.input: Current min=3, encode value=7, then decode
examples.trace:
Encoding: diff = 7-3 = 4 (positive, no min change)
Store: 4
Min remains: 3
Decoding: Retrieve diff=4
Since diff≥0: decoded value = min + diff = 3+4 = 7

ladders.title: Core Logic — Tracking Minimum Through Difference Sign
ladders.desc: Understanding how the sign of stored differences indicates minimum changes.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the central insight: the sign of the stored difference tells us whether we encountered a new minimum. A negative difference means the pushed value was smaller than the current minimum, so it becomes the new minimum. A non-negative difference means the pushed value was greater than or equal to the current minimum.

INTUITION: Instead of explicitly tracking minimum values separately, we embed this information in the sign of the differences we store. When we see a negative stored value during pop, we know we're removing what was the current minimum at that time, so we need to restore the previous minimum. This is done by subtracting the negative difference from the current minimum (which effectively adds its absolute value).

ALGORITHM:
Push logic:
- diff = new_value - current_min
- If diff < 0: we have a new minimum
  - Store diff (negative)
  - Update current_min = new_value
- Else: store diff (non-negative)

Pop logic:
- Retrieve stored diff
- If diff < 0: we're removing the current minimum
  - Restore previous min: current_min = current_min - diff
  (Since diff is negative, this adds the absolute value)

TIME/SPACE: O(1) for checking sign and updating.

CONNECTION TO L1: This sign-based tracking enables the encoding/decoding in L1 to work correctly.

COMMON MISTAKES:
1. Misinterpreting zero difference (means value equals current min)
2. Forgetting that negative diff during pop requires min restoration
3. Confusing when to update min during push vs pop

CODE PATTERN:
# During push
diff = val - current_min
if diff < 0:
    # New minimum found
    stack.append(diff)
    current_min = val
else:
    stack.append(diff)

# During pop
diff = stack.pop()
if diff < 0:
    # Removing current minimum, restore previous
    current_min = current_min - diff  # diff is negative, so this adds

examples.input: Push sequence: min=5, push 3, push 7, push 2
examples.trace:
Start: min=5
Push 3: diff=3-5=-2 (<0) → store -2, update min=3
Push 7: diff=7-3=4 (≥0) → store 4, min stays 3
Push 2: diff=2-3=-1 (<0) → store -1, update min=2
Final: stack contains [-2,4,-1], min=2

examples.input: Pop sequence from above: pop, pop, pop
examples.trace:
Current: stack=[-2,4,-1], min=2
Pop: diff=-1 (<0) → restore min=2-(-1)=3
Pop: diff=4 (≥0) → no min change, min=3
Pop: diff=-2 (<0) → restore min=3-(-2)=5
Final: stack empty, min=5

testCases.input: min=5, push 3, push 7, push 1, get diffs
min=0, push -5, push 5, push -10, get diffs

ladders.title: Building Block — Integer Arithmetic with Overflow Considerations
ladders.desc: Handling the arithmetic operations and potential overflow when calculating differences.
ladders.explanation:
This building block addresses the mathematical operations needed for the difference approach, particularly focusing on the subtraction and addition operations and how to handle potential integer overflow.

INTUITION: The core operation is calculating val - current_min. This subtraction must work correctly for all integer values, including negative numbers. When we have large numbers, we need to ensure we don't encounter integer overflow. The reconstruction operation current_min + diff must also handle all cases correctly.

ALGORITHM:
1. Difference calculation: diff = val - current_min
   - Handles all sign combinations correctly
   - Example: current_min=5, val=3 → diff=3-5=-2
   - Example: current_min=-5, val=3 → diff=3-(-5)=8

2. Value reconstruction: original = current_min + diff
   - Verifies: current_min + (val - current_min) = val
   - Works for all cases

3. Minimum restoration: new_min = current_min - diff (when diff<0)
   - Since diff is negative: current_min - (negative) = current_min + |diff|
   - Example: current_min=3, diff=-2 → 3-(-2)=5

OVERFLOW CONSIDERATIONS:
- Use appropriate integer types (64-bit for large numbers)
- The operations are safe because we're storing differences, not extremely large numbers
- Worst case: diff could be large if val and current_min have opposite signs and large magnitudes

TIME/SPACE: O(1) for arithmetic operations.

CONNECTION TO L2: These arithmetic operations implement the sign-based tracking logic from L2.

COMMON MISTAKES:
1. Using wrong order: val - min, not min - val
2. Not handling negative numbers correctly in subtraction
3. Forgetting that restoring min uses subtraction of negative diff

CODE PATTERN:
# Safe difference calculation
def calculate_diff(val, current_min):
    return val - current_min  # Handles all cases

# Safe value reconstruction
def reconstruct_value(diff, current_min):
    if diff < 0:
        return current_min  # Was the minimum
    return current_min + diff

# Safe minimum restoration
def restore_min(diff, current_min):
    # Only called when diff < 0
    return current_min - diff  # diff negative, so adds

examples.input: Calculate: val=3, current_min=5
examples.trace:
diff = val - current_min = 3 - 5 = -2
Reconstruction check: current_min + diff = 5 + (-2) = 3 ✓

examples.input: Calculate: val=-5, current_min=-3, then restore min with diff=-2
examples.trace:
diff = -5 - (-3) = -5 + 3 = -2
After push, current_min becomes -5 (new minimum).
Restoration on pop: previous_min = current_min - diff = -5 - (-2) = -5 + 2 = -3
Verification: Original min before pushing -5 was -3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push and Pop with Value Tracking
ladders.desc: Implementing basic stack operations while maintaining an additional tracked value.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the fundamental stack operations (push and pop) enhanced to maintain an additional piece of state—in this case, a tracked value that represents some important information about the stack contents.

INTUITION: A regular stack only stores values. We need to extend this to also track additional information (which will eventually be the minimum value). This teaches the pattern of maintaining auxiliary state alongside stack operations, which is crucial for the min stack solution.

ALGORITHM:
Basic stack with tracked value:
1. Initialize: stack = empty list, tracked_value = None
2. Push(val):
   - Append val to stack
   - Update tracked_value based on some rule
3. Pop():
   - Remove top element from stack
   - Update tracked_value if needed
4. Get tracked value: return tracked_value

SPECIFIC RULE FOR MIN STACK CONTEXT:
The tracked value is the minimum. Update rules:
- On push: if stack was empty or val < tracked_value, update tracked_value = val
- On pop: if popped value equals tracked_value, we need to find new minimum

TIME/SPACE: O(1) for push/pop if we don't search for new min on pop. O(n) if we search naively.

CONNECTION TO L3: This basic operation pattern will be enhanced in L3 with the difference arithmetic to avoid O(n) search on pop.

COMMON MISTAKES:
1. Forgetting to initialize tracked_value
2. Not updating tracked_value correctly on push
3. Not handling empty stack case

CODE PATTERN:
class StackWithTrackedValue:
    def __init__(self):
        self.stack = []
        self.tracked = None  # Will track minimum
    
    def push(self, val):
        self.stack.append(val)
        if self.tracked is None or val < self.tracked:
            self.tracked = val
    
    def pop(self):
        if not self.stack:
            return None
        val = self.stack.pop()
        if val == self.tracked:
            # Need to find new minimum - O(n) operation
            self.tracked = min(self.stack) if self.stack else None
        return val
    
    def getTracked(self):
        return self.tracked

examples.input: Push 5, push 3, push 7, get tracked, pop, get tracked
examples.output: Tracked after pushes: 3, tracked after pop: 3
examples.trace:
Initialize: stack=[], tracked=None
push(5): stack=[5], tracked=5 (first element)
push(3): stack=[5,3], 3<5 so tracked=3
push(7): stack=[5,3,7], 7≥3 so tracked stays 3
getTracked(): return 3
pop(): remove 7, stack=[5,3], 7≠3 so tracked stays 3
getTracked(): return 3

examples.input: Push 2, push 0, push 3, push 0, get tracked, pop, get tracked
examples.output: Tracked after pushes: 0, tracked after pop: 0
examples.trace:
Initialize: stack=[], tracked=None
push(2): stack=[2], tracked=2
push(0): stack=[2,0], 0<2 so tracked=0
push(3): stack=[2,0,3], 3≥0 so tracked stays 0
push(0): stack=[2,0,3,0], 0=0 so tracked stays 0
getTracked(): return 0
pop(): remove 0, stack=[2,0,3], popped 0 equals tracked, find new min=0
getTracked(): return 0

testCases.input: push(5), push(3), push(1), pop(), pop(), getTracked()

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Basic Stack Operations and Minimum Finding
ladders.desc: Understanding fundamental stack operations and how to find minimum in a collection.
ladders.explanation:
This foundation level covers the absolute basics: what a stack is, its core operations, and the simple approach to finding a minimum value. This is essential prerequisite knowledge for understanding why the min stack problem is challenging and why we need clever solutions.

INTUITION: A stack is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure. The basic operations are push (add to top), pop (remove from top), and peek/top (view top without removing). Finding the minimum in a collection typically requires examining all elements, which takes O(n) time. The challenge in the min stack problem is to do this in O(1) time while maintaining stack semantics.

STACK OPERATIONS:
1. Push(item): Add item to the top of the stack
2. Pop(): Remove and return the top item
3. Top()/Peek(): Return the top item without removing it
4. isEmpty(): Check if stack is empty

FINDING MINIMUM:
The naive approach to find minimum in a stack:
- Iterate through all elements: O(n) time
- Keep track of smallest seen so far

EXAMPLE:
Stack: [5, 3, 7, 2]
Find min: look at 5 (min=5), then 3 (min=3), then 7 (min=3), then 2 (min=2)
Result: 2

TIME COMPLEXITY:
- Push: O(1)
- Pop: O(1)
- Top: O(1)
- getMin (naive): O(n)

SPACE COMPLEXITY: O(n) for storing n elements.

CONNECTION TO L4: This basic understanding leads to recognizing the need for tracking minimum during operations rather than searching each time.

COMMON MISTAKES:
1. Confusing stack (LIFO) with queue (FIFO)
2. Trying to access middle elements of stack (not allowed)
3. Forgetting to check empty stack before pop/top

CODE PATTERN:
class SimpleStack:
    def __init__(self):
        self.items = []
    
    def push(self, item):
        self.items.append(item)
    
    def pop(self):
        if self.isEmpty():
            return None
        return self.items.pop()
    
    def top(self):
        if self.isEmpty():
            return None
        return self.items[-1]
    
    def isEmpty(self):
        return len(self.items) == 0

# Naive min finding
def find_min(stack):
    if stack.isEmpty():
        return None
    min_val = stack.top()
    # Would need to pop all elements to check them
    # This shows why we need a better approach

examples.input: Push 5, push 3, push 7, top, pop, top
examples.trace:
Start: stack=[]
push(5): stack=[5]
push(3): stack=[5,3]
push(7): stack=[5,3,7]
top(): return 7 (top element)
pop(): remove 7, stack=[5,3]
top(): return 3 (new top)

examples.input: Find minimum in stack [5, 3, 7, 2]
examples.trace:
Stack elements: 5, 3, 7, 2
Examine each:
- 5: current min=5
- 3: 3<5, current min=3
- 7: 7≥3, current min=3
- 2: 2<3, current min=2
Final minimum: 2

testCases.input: push(5), push(3), push(7), pop(), pop(), top()

approaches.name: Two Stacks with Auxiliary O(n) extra space
approaches.summary: Maintain two stacks: one for all elements and another for minimums. Push and pop update both stacks to track the current minimum efficiently.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) extra space for auxiliary stack

ladders.title: Full Problem — Min Stack with Two Stacks Approach
ladders.desc: Implement a stack that supports push, pop, top, and getMin operations in O(1) time using two stacks.
ladders.explanation: The Min Stack problem requires maintaining a stack data structure that supports standard operations (push, pop, top) while also providing O(1) access to the current minimum element. The two-stacks approach solves this by maintaining two separate stacks: one for all elements (main stack) and another specifically for tracking minimums (min stack). The intuition is that at any point, the top of the min stack always represents the current minimum value in the entire stack. When pushing a new element, we compare it with the current minimum (top of min stack). If it's less than or equal to the current minimum, we push it onto both stacks. This ensures the min stack always has the smallest element at the top. When popping, we check if the element being removed from the main stack equals the top of the min stack. If so, we also pop from the min stack, maintaining the invariant that the min stack's top always represents the current minimum. The time complexity is O(1) for all operations because each operation involves at most two stack operations (push/pop on both stacks). Space complexity is O(n) in the worst case when all elements are in non-increasing order, requiring all elements to be stored in the min stack. This approach is efficient and straightforward, making it ideal for interview scenarios where clarity and correctness are prioritized over space optimization. Common mistakes include not handling equal minimum values correctly (using 'less than or equal to' instead of just 'less than' when pushing to min stack) and forgetting to check for empty stacks during pop or getMin operations. The connection to simpler concepts is direct: it builds on basic stack operations and the idea of maintaining auxiliary data structures to track additional information.

examples.input: push(3), push(5), getMin(), push(2), push(1), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
examples.output: null, null, 3, null, null, 1, null, 2, null, 3
examples.trace:
Step 1: push(3) - main=[3], min=[3]
Step 2: push(5) - main=[3,5], min=[3] (5 > 3, so only push to main)
Step 3: getMin() - returns 3 (top of min stack)
Step 4: push(2) - main=[3,5,2], min=[3,2] (2 <= 3, push to both)
Step 5: push(1) - main=[3,5,2,1], min=[3,2,1] (1 <= 2, push to both)
Step 6: getMin() - returns 1
Step 7: pop() - remove 1 from main, main=[3,5,2], since 1 == top of min stack, pop min too, min=[3,2]
Step 8: getMin() - returns 2
Step 9: pop() - remove 2 from main, main=[3,5], pop from min, min=[3]
Step 10: getMin() - returns 3

examples.input: push(10), push(20), getMin(), push(5), getMin(), push(5), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
examples.output: null, null, 10, null, 5, null, 5, null, 5, null, 10
examples.trace:
Step 1: push(10) - main=[10], min=[10]
Step 2: push(20) - main=[10,20], min=[10]
Step 3: getMin() - returns 10
Step 4: push(5) - main=[10,20,5], min=[10,5] (5 <= 10)
Step 5: getMin() - returns 5
Step 6: push(5) - main=[10,20,5,5], min=[10,5,5] (5 <= 5, push to both)
Step 7: getMin() - returns 5
Step 8: pop() - remove 5 from main, main=[10,20,5], pop from min (5 == top), min=[10,5]
Step 9: getMin() - returns 5
Step 10: pop() - remove 5 from main, main=[10,20], pop from min, min=[10]
Step 11: getMin() - returns 10

testCases.input: push(1), push(2), push(3), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(5), push(3), push(4), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(2), push(2), push(1), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(7), push(8), push(6), push(9), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
testCases.expected: null, null, null, null, 6, null, 6, null, 7

testCases.input: push(0), push(-1), push(-2), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(100), push(50), push(75), push(25), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
testCases.expected: null, null, null, null, 25, null, 50, null, 50

testCases.input: push(3), push(3), push(3), getMin(), pop(), getMin(), pop(), getMin()
push(1), push(2), getMin(), push(0), getMin(), pop(), getMin()

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Push Operation with Min Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement the push operation that maintains the min stack invariant when adding new elements.
ladders.explanation: The push operation is the most critical subroutine in the two-stacks min stack approach because it ensures the min stack correctly tracks the current minimum. The intuition is that whenever we add a new element to the main stack, we need to update the min stack to reflect whether this new element becomes the new minimum or not. The algorithm works as follows: First, push the element onto the main stack. Then, check if the min stack is empty OR if the new element is less than or equal to the current minimum (which is the top of the min stack). If either condition is true, push the new element onto the min stack as well. The 'less than or equal to' condition is crucial—it handles duplicate minimum values correctly. If we only used 'less than', duplicate minima wouldn't be tracked, and popping one duplicate would incorrectly remove the minimum from the min stack even though another duplicate remains. This operation takes O(1) time since it involves at most two stack pushes. Space complexity adds O(1) per push in the worst case (when the new element is a new minimum or equal to current minimum). This subroutine directly enables the O(1) getMin operation because it maintains the invariant that the min stack's top is always the current minimum. Common mistakes include using strict inequality (only <) which breaks with duplicates, or forgetting to check if the min stack is empty for the first element. The connection to the full problem is that this push logic, combined with the corresponding pop logic, creates the complete min stack implementation.

examples.input: Main stack: [3,5], Min stack: [3]. Push(2)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Push 2 to main stack → main becomes [3,5,2]
Step 2: Check min stack: top is 3, new element 2 <= 3? Yes
Step 3: Push 2 to min stack → min becomes [3,2]
Result: Both stacks updated, min stack now has 2 as top (current minimum)

examples.input: Main stack: [10,20,5], Min stack: [10,5]. Push(8)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Push 8 to main stack → main becomes [10,20,5,8]
Step 2: Check min stack: top is 5, new element 8 <= 5? No
Step 3: Do not push to min stack → min remains [10,5]
Result: Only main stack updated, min stack unchanged since 8 > current minimum 5

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Minimum with Auxiliary Stack
ladders.desc: Understand how an auxiliary stack tracks the current minimum independently of the main stack.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of the two-stacks approach is that we can maintain a separate stack (the min stack) that parallels the main stack but only contains elements that are or were minimums at some point. The intuition is that the minimum element can only change when we push a new smaller (or equal) element or pop the current minimum. The min stack cleverly stores these 'milestone' minimums. When pushing, if the new element is less than or equal to the current minimum (top of min stack), it becomes a new milestone and gets pushed onto the min stack. When popping, if the element being removed equals the current minimum (top of min stack), we pop from the min stack too, reverting to the previous minimum. This ensures that at any time, the top of the min stack is the minimum of all elements currently in the main stack. The key property is that the min stack is non-increasing (each element is <= the one below it), which matches how minima evolve as we push and pop. This logic works because stack operations are LIFO—the order of pushes and pops ensures we always remove the most recent minimum before older ones. Time complexity for maintaining this is O(1) per operation since we just check and possibly push/pop from the min stack. Space complexity is O(n) in worst case when elements are pushed in non-increasing order, causing every element to be stored in the min stack. This core logic is what enables O(1) getMin—we simply return the top of the min stack without any computation. Common mistakes include not understanding why we push equal elements to the min stack (to handle duplicates) or why the min stack can have fewer elements than the main stack. The connection to the key subroutine is that this logic is implemented in the push and pop operations.

examples.input: Push sequence: 5, 3, 7, 3, 1. Show min stack after each push.
examples.output:
After push(5): min=[5]
After push(3): min=[5,3]
After push(7): min=[5,3]
After push(3): min=[5,3,3]
After push(1): min=[5,3,3,1]
examples.trace:
Step 1: Push 5 → min stack empty, push 5: min=[5]
Step 2: Push 3 → current min=5, 3<=5? Yes, push 3: min=[5,3]
Step 3: Push 7 → current min=3, 7<=3? No, no push: min=[5,3]
Step 4: Push 3 → current min=3, 3<=3? Yes, push 3: min=[5,3,3]
Step 5: Push 1 → current min=3, 1<=3? Yes, push 1: min=[5,3,3,1]
At each step, top of min stack is current minimum: 5,3,3,3,1

examples.input: Start with main=[5,3,7,3,1], min=[5,3,3,1]. Pop twice, show min stack after each pop.
examples.output:
After first pop: min=[5,3,3]
After second pop: min=[5,3]
examples.trace:
Step 1: Pop from main → removes 1 from main
Check: 1 equals top of min stack (1)? Yes, pop from min too
Min stack becomes [5,3,3]
Step 2: Pop from main → removes 3 from main
Check: 3 equals top of min stack (3)? Yes, pop from min too
Min stack becomes [5,3]
After pops, top of min stack (3) is minimum of remaining elements [5,3,7,3]

testCases.input: Push: 4,2,6,2,0. Min stack after all pushes?
Min stack: [10,5,5,1]. Pop from main removes 1. New min stack?
Min stack: [8,4]. Pop from main removes 6. New min stack?
Min stack: [5,2,2]. Pop from main removes 2. New min stack?
Min stack: [0,-1,-1]. Pop from main removes -1. New min stack?
Min stack: [7,7]. Pop from main removes 7. New min stack?

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Data Structure Implementation
ladders.desc: Implement basic stack operations (push, pop, top, isEmpty) using a list.
ladders.explanation: A stack is a fundamental data structure that follows Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) order. It supports core operations: push (add element to top), pop (remove and return top element), top/peek (return top element without removal), and isEmpty (check if stack is empty). Implementing a stack using a list (dynamic array) is straightforward and efficient for most purposes. The intuition is that we can use the end of the list as the top of the stack—appending to the list simulates push, and removing from the end simulates pop, both O(1) amortized operations. The algorithm involves maintaining a list and using list methods: push uses append(), pop uses pop() (which removes and returns last element), top uses indexing (list[-1] for last element), and isEmpty checks if list length is 0. Time complexity is O(1) for all operations assuming list append/pop is O(1) amortized. Space complexity is O(n) where n is number of elements. This building block is essential because the min stack approach uses two such stacks. Common mistakes include using the wrong end of the list (beginning instead of end) which makes push/pop O(n), or not handling empty stack cases in top/pop (which should raise errors or return sentinel values). The connection to the core logic is that we need two independent stacks to implement the min tracking—one for all elements and one for minimums.

examples.input: Operations: push(5), push(3), top(), pop(), top(), isEmpty()
examples.output:
After push(5): stack=[5]
After push(3): stack=[5,3]
top() returns 3
pop() returns 3, stack=[5]
top() returns 5
isEmpty() returns False
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize empty list []
Step 2: push(5) → append 5, list becomes [5]
Step 3: push(3) → append 3, list becomes [5,3]
Step 4: top() → return last element list[-1]=3
Step 5: pop() → remove and return last element (3), list becomes [5]
Step 6: top() → return last element list[-1]=5
Step 7: isEmpty() → check length, len([5])=1 > 0, return False

examples.input: Operations: push(10), pop(), isEmpty(), push(20), top()
examples.output:
After push(10): stack=[10]
pop() returns 10, stack=[]
isEmpty() returns True
After push(20): stack=[20]
top() returns 20
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with []
Step 2: push(10) → append 10, list=[10]
Step 3: pop() → remove and return 10, list=[]
Step 4: isEmpty() → len([])=0, return True
Step 5: push(20) → append 20, list=[20]
Step 6: top() → return list[-1]=20

testCases.input: push(1), push(2), push(3), pop(), pop(), push(4), top()
testCases.expected: First pop=3, second pop=2, top=4, stack=[1,4]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Updating Minimum Value
ladders.desc: Given current minimum and new element, determine if minimum should update and how.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing a new element with the current minimum value and deciding whether to update the minimum. The intuition is simple: a minimum is the smallest value in a collection, so when we encounter a new element, if it's smaller than the current minimum, it becomes the new minimum. If it's equal to the current minimum, we might need to track it depending on context (for stacks with duplicates, we treat it as a new minimum for tracking purposes). The algorithm is: given current_min and new_element, if new_element < current_min, then new_minimum = new_element. If new_element == current_min, in many tracking scenarios (like our min stack), we consider it as equaling the minimum and update our tracking. If new_element > current_min, the minimum remains unchanged. This operation is O(1) time and O(1) space. It's fundamental because the min stack approach relies on this comparison during push operations to decide whether to push to the min stack. Common mistakes include using <= instead of < when equality should be ignored, or vice versa. The connection to the building block is that this comparison happens inside stack push operations.

examples.input: Current min: 5, New element: 3. Should minimum update?
examples.trace:
Step 1: Compare 3 and 5
Step 2: 3 < 5 is true
Step 3: Therefore minimum should update from 5 to 3

examples.input: Current min: 2, New element: 2. Should we track as new minimum?
examples.trace:
Step 1: Compare 2 and 2
Step 2: 2 == 2 is true
Step 3: For min stack, we treat this as equal to minimum and track it (push to min stack)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Minimum in a Collection
ladders.desc: Find the smallest element in a set of numbers.
ladders.explanation: The concept of a minimum is foundational in mathematics and computer science: given a collection of numbers, the minimum is the smallest value among them. For example, in the set {5, 2, 8, 1}, the minimum is 1 because it's less than all other numbers. The intuition is straightforward—we compare numbers using the less-than operator (<). To find the minimum, we can initialize a variable with the first element (or a very large number if collection is empty), then iterate through all elements, updating this variable whenever we find a smaller value. This is essentially what the min() function does in programming languages. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of elements, since we need to check each element once. Space complexity is O(1) for just storing the current minimum. This concept is the bedrock for the min stack problem—the entire challenge is maintaining this minimum efficiently as elements are added and removed. Common mistakes include not handling empty collections (there is no minimum), or comparing incorrectly (e.g., using > instead of <). The connection to the basic operation is that updating a minimum as new elements arrive builds directly on this foundation.

examples.input: Numbers: [3, 7, 2, 9]. Find minimum.
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with first number 3 as current min
Step 2: Compare with 7: 7 < 3? No, min remains 3
Step 3: Compare with 2: 2 < 3? Yes, update min to 2
Step 4: Compare with 9: 9 < 2? No, min remains 2
Final minimum is 2

examples.input: Numbers: [5, 5, 5]. Find minimum.
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with first number 5 as current min
Step 2: Compare with second 5: 5 < 5? No, min remains 5
Step 3: Compare with third 5: 5 < 5? No, min remains 5
All numbers equal, minimum is 5
